subject: Fw: BrainMusic Public Education: Questions From A High School Student (Washington State)
body:
----- Forwarded Message ----
From: Lesley Groff <lesley@nysgllc.com>
To: J. Epstein <jeeproject@yahoo.com>
Sent: Tuesday, January 2, 2007 9:42:55 AM
Subject: FW: BrainMusic Public Education: Questions From A High School Student (Washington State)

BrainMusic Public Education: Questions From A High School Student (Washington State)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Lesley Groff

Executive Assistant to Jeffrey 
Epstein

 

 




From: Tramo, Mark Jude 
[mailto:mark_tramo@hms.harvard.edu] 
Sent: Tuesday, January 02, 2007 
9:31 AM
To: AAnde15875@aol.com
Subject: BrainMusic Public 
Education: Questions From A High School Student (Washington 
State)





Dear Nate,

Thank you for your email - how wonderful that 
you are thinking about experiments - the source of empirical knowledge - at such 
an early age!

When you do an experiment, you need a set of methods that 
deal with:  1) who you are going to study (perhaps yourself and a few 
classmates);  2) what the musical backgrounds of your experimental subjects 
are;  3) what musical stimuli you will use (perhaps one that moves you and 
one that doesn't); and 4) what responses you will measure (perhaps rating how 
"emotional" a stimulus makes you feel on a scale of 1 to 10, using a 
standardized answer sheet, like those in school).

You asked good 
questions below.  Now do an experiment on music and emotion, following the 
above hints - and have lots of fun with it, like most of us brain scientists 
when we do experiments on music!

Please visit the"Auditory Neuroscience" 
and "Educational Activities" pages at www.brainmusic.org - follow the links on 
the latter page to my Harvard course syllabus and selected readings, which 
include some papers on music and emotion.

Sincerely,
Mark Jude Tramo, 
MD, PhD
Director, The Institute for Music & Brain Science
Dept of 
Neurology, Harvard Medical School & Massachusetts General 
Hospital
Steering Committee, Harvard University Mind/Brain/Behavior 
Interfaculty Initiative
Best Doctors in America®/America’s Top Doctors®/Best 
of Boston®
Songwriter Member, ASCAP

Mailing Address:  175 
Cambridge St, Suite 340, Boston, MA 02114 USA
Email  
MTramo@HMS.Harvard.edu
URL  http://www.BrainMusic.org

END

-----Original 
Message-----
From:   AAnde15875@aol.com [mailto:AAnde15875@aol.com]
Sent:   
Sun 10/1/2006 2:04 PM
To:     Tramo, Mark 
Jude
Cc:    
Subject:        
Questions From A High School Student

My name is Nate and I'm freshman at 
Gig  Harbor High in Washington state. I'm
very interested in your field 
of study,  about how music affects the brain. I
am a musician myself, so 
I find this topic  fascinating. I have to do a
project called "Designing 
an Experiment" on this  topic, so I was wondering what
some of the 
important questions asked by  scientists are in this field. I listen
to 
music most of my day, and I was  wondering if it matters what style 
of
music I listen to. Music affects my moods,  and how focused I am on 
any sort of
project. Honestly, right now I am listening  to music. I can 
enjoy any type of
music, but I mostly like classic rock, and  anything 
that takes some talent
and time to create. My last question is, how  
does being a musician affects the
brain positively?


Thanks for 
your time


sincerely,

Nate Anderson




P.S. I 
get some extra credit points if you respond to this  
email










__________________________________________________
Do You Yahoo!?
Tired of spam?  Yahoo! Mail has the best spam protection around 
http://mail.yahoo.com

Dear Nate,

Thank you for your email - how wonderful that you are thinking about experiments - the source of empirical knowledge - at such an early age!

When you do an experiment, you need a set of methods that deal with:  1) who you are going to study (perhaps yourself and a few classmates);  2) what the musical backgrounds of your experimental subjects are;  3) what musical stimuli you will use (perhaps one that moves you and one that doesn't); and 4) what responses you will measure (perhaps rating how "emotional" a stimulus makes you feel on a scale of 1 to 10, using a standardized answer sheet, like those in school).

You asked good questions below.  Now do an experiment on music and emotion, following the above hints - and have lots of fun with it, like most of us brain scientists when we do experiments on music!

Please visit the"Auditory Neuroscience" and "Educational Activities" pages at www.brainmusic.org - follow the links on the latter page to my Harvard course syllabus and selected readings, which include some papers on music and emotion.

Sincerely,
Mark Jude Tramo, MD, PhD

From:   AAnde15875@aol.com [mailto:AAnde15875@aol.com]
Sent:   Sun 10/1/2006 2:04 PM
To:     Tramo, Mark Jude
Cc:    
Subject:        Questions From A High School Student

My name is Nate and I'm freshman at Gig  Harbor High in Washington state. I'm
very interested in your field of study,  about how music affects the brain. I
am a musician myself, so I find this topic  fascinating. I have to do a
project called "Designing an Experiment" on this  topic, so I was wondering what
some of the important questions asked by  scientists are in this field. I listen
to music most of my day, and I was  wondering if it matters what style of
music I listen to. Music affects my moods,  and how focused I am on any sort of
project. Honestly, right now I am listening  to music. I can enjoy any type of
music, but I mostly like classic rock, and  anything that takes some talent
and time to create. My last question is, how  does being a musician affects the
brain positively?


Thanks for your time


sincerely,

Nate Anderson




P.S. I get some extra credit points if you respond to this  email
